text: In this video from Oxford and the Royal Marsden in the United Kingdom, we present the key steps in anterior retsia sparing radical prostatectomy. The concept of anterior retsia sparing is to retain the essential elements of conventional posterior retsia sparing, which are important for functional outcomes, while maintaining the anterior approach, which is easier for training and adoption. There are five key steps to this approach and we will work through each in turn. The first step, which is not really fundamental to anterioretsia sparing as it does not impact on functional outcomes, but is helpful for keeping the bladder retracted out of the pelvis, is to maintain the urechial attachment so that the bladder remains tethered to the umbilicus. We divide the peritoneum in the usual fashion down to a pre-marked point where the median umbilical ligament crosses the vas deferens. The next landmark is of course the bone of the pubic arch with a sweeping movement, often all that is required to clear this off. The same maneuver on the other side with the peritoneum divided down to the pre-marked point. and then the retzius space partially opened towards the midline in order to join up with the space created on the contralateral side. As mentioned, we leave the more proximal attachments tethered to the umbilicus, but it is helpful to release some of these fibers to create space. The fourth arm then comes in to retract the bladder down and right, creating a window for the camera and assistants to advance towards the pelvis. Next we have the first key component of anteriorepsia sparing, the preservation of the endopelvic fascia. In smaller prostates such as this one, it can make delineating the anterior base of the prostate harder. But good tension with the fourth arm, pulling on the catheter and compression with the bipolar forceps all help with this. After switching to a thirty degree down scope, the anterior bladder neck is divided directly without prior opening of the endopelvic fascia as shown here.

The surgeon performs a unilateral opening of the endopelvic fascia on the left side, leaving the right side closed to maintain pelvic floor tension. The anterior base of the prostate is defined by the curvature visible from the open endopelvic fascia. The anterior bladder neck dissection continues, followed by a modified nerve-sparing technique due to the closed endopelvic fascia. Hemlock clips are applied to release the dorsolateral vascular pedicle at the base. The fourth arm rotates the prostate out of the pelvis, aiding in the nerve-sparing procedure, which is performed in an antegrade fashion. A retrograde approach is also demonstrated, with hemlock clips bridging the gap on the dorsolateral pedicle.

The surgeon corrects the dissection from the interfacial to the intrafacial plane, peeling off a closer layer before proceeding to the anterior fibromuscular tissue. The anterior apron is released from the base of the prostate using monopolar diathermy, maintaining distance from the neurovascular bundle. The prostate is retracted dorsally and cranially, and heat is applied to hug the anterior fibromuscular surface, minimizing the opening of DVC vessels. Cold cutting with scissors is also used to advance under the DVC with minimal vessel opening, aided by a clean ventral lateral dissection on the right side.

The surgeon proceeds towards the urethra at the apex of the prostate, aiming to preserve maximal urethral length. The urethra is sharply divided, revealing the verumontanum within the prosthetic urethra. The prostate is then removed and placed in an endocatch bag, leaving a prostatic bed that appears shell-like. The DVC is visible but does not require oversewing.

here is the shelled out egg appearance of the prostate ex vivo and pleasingly this man had negative margins was dry from removal of catheter and had some erections although insufficient for intercourse at initial follow-up furthermore the psa was zero point zero one and the margin tissue did not contain any prostate glands Here we present an overview of outcomes for two hundred and eight cases, the majority of which were performed at the Royal Marsden Hospital in London. Overall, just under sixty percent were dry, meaning no pads whatsoever just after catheter removal. The remainder all managed with one pad. The positive margin rate was just over twenty percent. Finally, a small number of men with negative margins did have a persistent PSA consistent with retained benign tissue. This video has been brought to you by urologists from Oxford and the Royal Marsden Hospital in the United Kingdom. Thank you very much for watching
